branchingScenario.title: AN29.1-AN30.5 | Posterior triangle of neck — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenTitle: Posterior Triangle — Boundaries, Brachial Plexus & Clinical Correlations
startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A newborn is noticed to have the head tilted to one side. Work through this case exploring the posterior triangle boundaries, brachial plexus injuries, and accessory nerve anatomy.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified torticollis, posterior triangle anatomy, and brachial plexus injury patterns.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review SCM, brachial plexus roots, and the accessory nerve.
Revisit the posterior triangle boundaries, Erb's palsy presentation, scalene muscles, and accessory nerve course.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A newborn is noticed to have the head persistently tilted to the right with the chin rotated to the opposite (left) side. A firm swelling is palpable in the right sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle. This is congenital torticollis (wryneck), caused by fibrosis of the SCM.

The posterior triangle of the neck is bounded anteriorly by the sternocleidomastoid, posteriorly by the trapezius, and inferiorly by the middle third of the clavicle.

The brachial plexus roots emerge between the scalenus anterior and scalenus medius muscles in the posterior triangle.

branchingQuestion.question: Another newborn in the nursery had a difficult delivery with shoulder dystocia. The baby's right arm hangs limply with the shoulder adducted and elbow extended — the waiter's tip position. Which brachial plexus injury is this?

alternatives.text: Erb's palsy — upper trunk injury (C5, C6) causing the waiter's tip position
Klumpke's palsy — lower trunk injury (C8, T1) causing a claw hand

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Klumpke's palsy affects the lower trunk (C8, T1) and causes a claw hand, not the waiter's tip. The waiter's tip position (adducted shoulder, extended elbow) is Erb's palsy (C5, C6 — upper trunk).

alternatives.text: Complete brachial plexus palsy affecting all roots C5-T1

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Complete plexus palsy would cause a flail arm. The specific waiter's tip position (adducted shoulder, extended elbow, pronated forearm) is characteristic of Erb's palsy — upper trunk (C5, C6) injury.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Erb's palsy is an upper trunk (C5, C6) injury producing the waiter's tip position.

Returning to the original patient, you plan physiotherapy for the torticollis. You counsel the parents that the accessory nerve (spinal accessory, CN XI) crosses the posterior triangle obliquely and is at risk during surgical procedures such as lymph node biopsy in this region.

The accessory nerve supplies two key muscles: the sternocleidomastoid and the trapezius. Damage during surgery would cause weakness of shoulder shrugging (trapezius) and difficulty turning the head (SCM).

branchingQuestion.question: A patient undergoes lymph node biopsy in the posterior triangle and develops weakness of shoulder shrugging. Which nerve was likely damaged?

alternatives.text: The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI), which crosses the posterior triangle and supplies trapezius
The phrenic nerve, which crosses the anterior surface of scalenus anterior

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The phrenic nerve lies on scalenus anterior and supplies the diaphragm, not the trapezius. Shoulder shrug weakness indicates accessory nerve (CN XI) damage.

alternatives.text: The long thoracic nerve, which supplies serratus anterior

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Long thoracic nerve damage causes winging of the scapula (serratus anterior weakness), not shoulder shrug weakness. The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) supplies trapezius.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key posterior triangle anatomy:

  • Boundaries: SCM (anterior), trapezius (posterior), clavicle (inferior)
  • Torticollis: SCM fibrosis — head tilts to affected side, chin rotates to opposite side
  • Erb's palsy: Upper trunk (C5, C6) — waiter's tip position
  • Accessory nerve (XI): Crosses triangle obliquely; supplies SCM + trapezius; at risk during biopsy
  • Brachial plexus roots: Emerge between scalenus anterior and medius
